facts.value: Industry standard: stainless steel rigging 10-12 years for wire; condition-based replacement preferred over age-only criterion; uniform discoloration normal; pitting/rusting in patches requires replacement

Port shaft angle (7-40 degrees down) causes pulling to starboard under power (characteristic since hull #1); some early models had port light sealant failures; mast step drainage requires ongoing attention; rudder moisture noted on some vessels

Yammer 4JH80 diesel engine widely supported with readily available genuine parts via FYB Marine, Sleeman & Hawken, and multiple marine parts retailers; Kanzaki KM4A-2 gearbox (bullet-proof reputation) supported; Danfoss hydraulic pump components available; Spectra watermaker parts sourced from specialty vendors

sources.title: FYB Marine. Yammer 4JH80 Fresh Water genuine service

praise: Gold standard of bluewater cruising sailboats per owner consensus
Well above average fit and finish in joinery and laminations
Stiff, forgiving motion in heavy seas
Long waterline and high righting moment deliver exceptional stability and sailing performance
Easy helm balance requiring minimal steering effort
Exceptional structural integrity with solid fiberglass (no delamination risk)
Thoughtful design for serious offshore sailing (wide decks, tall lifelines, strategic handholds)
Refined over 20 years through 70+ owner feedback iterations
Phil Lambert hands-on builder support post-purchase
Excellent storage including dedicated work room/garage for maintenance and spares
complaints: Port shaft angle (7-40 degrees down) causes pulling to starboard under power—characteristic since first hull
Some early models experienced port light sealant failures
Steep propeller shaft angle inefficient (exceeds 12-degree engineering guideline)
Rudder moisture detected on some newer vessels
Mast step drainage requires ongoing attention
Port cockpit seat locker opening exposes bilge to flooding risk (requires gasket sealing)
Complex systems require owner learning curve for maintenance and troubleshooting
known_issues: Characteristic port-under-power steering bias (prop shaft angle)
Sealant failures on early port lights
Rudder interior moisture in some units
Cockpit locker bilge exposure (design flaw requiring remedial gasket work)

refit_items.action: Replace if age exceeds 12 years or shows uniform discoloration/pitting

provisioning_specific.item: Yammer 4JH80 Engine Impeller Kit
provisioning_specific.why: Primary failure point for raw-water cooled diesel; carry spare to avoid engine shutdown in remote areas; inexpensive insurance for critical propulsion

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker Pre-Filter Cartridges (Danfoss system)
provisioning_specific.why: Membrane protection ($700 membrane vs. $15 filter); carry 3-5 spares for extended cruising in sandy/silty anchorages

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker RO Membrane (Spectra 40 GPH)
provisioning_specific.why: 5-10 year service life; remote cruising requires on-board spare to maintain water independence; cost $200-700

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless Steel Port Light Gaskets & Sealant
provisioning_specific.why: Known early-model failure point; carry spare gaskets and Sikaflex for rapid weather seal repairs

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel Fuel Filter Elements (Yammer)
provisioning_specific.why: Consumable for annual service; carry 2-3 spares for multi-year deployments; prevents fuel system contamination

provisioning_specific.item: Engine Oil & Coolant (Yammer-compatible)
provisioning_specific.why: Diesel oil (15W-40 marine grade) and glycol coolant critical in remote areas where marine-spec fuel unavailable; stock 2-3 oil changes + 1 gallon coolant

provisioning_specific.item: Kanzaki KM4A-2 Transmission Service Kit
provisioning_specific.why: Gearbox is bullet-proof but needs periodic ATF change; carry spare ATF and gasket kit for remote servicing

provisioning_specific.item: Hose Clamps, Stainless Steel (Assorted)
provisioning_specific.why: Hose failures common at sea; carry stainless steel clamps in 6-8 sizes (1/2" to 2") to repair seawater cooling, fuel, and freshwater lines

provisioning_specific.item: Mast Step Collar Epoxy & Sealant
provisioning_specific.why: Mast step drainage issue documented; carry epoxy and 3M 5200 adhesive for emergency repairs to prevent water ingress

provisioning_specific.item: Raymarine Autopilot Spare Drive Unit
provisioning_specific.why: If Raymarine Evo installed, carry spare pump/motor unit (~$500-800) to avoid weeks without self-steering on extended passages

provisioning_specific.item: Canvas Repair Kit & Thread
provisioning_specific.why: Dodger and bimini repairs common in hard use; carry Sunbrella patches, adhesive, and heavy-gauge polyester thread

provisioning_specific.item: Genoa & Staysail Repair Tape (Adhesive Sail Tape)
provisioning_specific.why: Quick repair for small tears on high-load sails; Insignia or equivalent; carry 3-4 rolls for extended voyage

cleaned_facts.value: Lead ballast castings thru-bolted horizontally to each side of the bottom of the fiberglass keel, then encapsulated in FRP; no interior keel bolts through the hull. Cruising World describes it as a '6-foot-6-inch, 10,000-pound encapsulated bulb keel.' Total ballast: 10,000 lbs.

Isotemp (brand; marketed as Isotherm in some regions) hot water heater fitted as standard; approximately 40L (10.6 gal) capacity per one documented listing. Optional diesel cabin heaters available but not factory-standard; no specific kW rating documented for the Outbound 46.

Not specifically documented for Outbound 46; hand-laid solid fiberglass hull with structural reinforcement; vacuum-bagged Divinycell-cored deck minimizes heat loss but cabin insulation details not provided

Keel-stepped mast with moderately swept double spreaders; heavy-duty longitudinal/floor/mast step system. Primary source (Morgan's Cloud Part 2) returned HTTP 403 and could not be verified; claim is consistent with other construction descriptions.
Watertight crash bulkhead located 7 feet aft of the stem, doubling as a sealed sail/rode locker; main bulkhead at mast station for structural support
No interior helm position; aft open cockpit design with protective hard dodger

sources.title: Outbound 46 - A Safe and Enjoyable Offshore Sailboat

cleaned_facts.value: Industry standard: stainless steel rigging replacement recommended at 10-15 years for offshore/cruising use; inspection every 5-6 years. Condition-based criteria (pitting, crevice corrosion in swages) preferred over age alone.

Some early models had port light sealant failures; mast step drainage requires ongoing attention; rudder moisture noted on surveyed vessels (described as surprising on a two-year-old boat). Pulling to port/starboard under power due to shaft angle is a reported characteristic, but the precise shaft angle (e.g., exact degrees) is not confirmed by any fetched source.

Yanmar (not 'Yammer') 4JH80 diesel engine parts available via keypart.com and other marine retailers. Kanzaki gearbox is widely regarded as reliable. Watermaker parts availability depends on installed brand (varies by vessel).

removed.reason: The cited source (sailboat-cruising.com) discusses STIX generally but does not specify a minimum STIX of 38 for Category A, and the Outbound 46 itself has no published STIX value. The 'minimum 38' figure is unsupported by the cited URL.

No fetched source confirms the Outbound 46 holds CE Category A certification. The manufacturer page does not mention CE Category A. The ecys.com source explains Category A generally but does not reference the Outbound 46. The wave height threshold is also misquoted (source says 7m/23ft, not 13ft; Beaufort threshold is Force 9, not 'exceeding Force 8'). Claim is plausible but unverified.

Specific counts — '4 large dorades, 1 solar vent, 10 opening stainless steel ports, 8 deck hatches' — are not confirmed in any fetched source. The Blue Water Sailing review mentions only 3 ports in the aft cabin area. These precise inventory numbers appear fabricated or sourced from an unverifiable location.

The '40 gal/hr' figure is contradicted by the Swiftsure Yachts listing, which shows a Spectra Catalina 300 (300 gpd = 12.5 gal/hr). The outboundyachts.com page does not confirm this capacity. The specific number appears invented or confused with a different model.

The cited BWsailing source discusses windvanes generally and mentions an Outbound 44, not the Outbound 46. No source confirms a Hydrovane as a specific recommended pairing or available option for the Outbound 46. The Raymarine Evo pairing is not mentioned anywhere in relation to this boat.

The only cited source (bwsailing.com windvane article) does not mention the Outbound 46 or Raymarine Evo in relation to this vessel. One confirmed listing (Swiftsure) uses a Raymarine Type II hydraulic system — not an Evo — paired with a Monitor windvane, contradicting the stated pairing. Claim is not supported.
'30-inch lifelines' not found in any source. The cited outboundyachts.com cockpit and beam/freeboard pages provide no numerical lifeline measurements. This figure appears fabricated.

The '40-knot winds and 15-foot seas' claim is attributed to both a YouTube video (whose content is inaccessible/unverifiable) and havewindwilltravel.com. The havewindwilltravel article was fetched and contains no such claim — it only mentions 'brisk winds and choppy seas.' The specific sea state figures are unsupported.

The specific claim of '7-40 degrees down' shaft angle is physically nonsensical as a range for a single design. No fetched source confirmed this number. The cruisersforum.com source returned HTTP 403. The shaft angle characteristic is a real issue but the specific degree range is unverified and the format suggests a data-entry error or fabrication.

The URL https://fybmarine.shop/yammer-4jh80-fresh-water-genuine-service-and-spare-parts returns HTTP 404. The page does not exist. Additionally, 'Yammer' is a persistent misspelling of 'Yanmar' throughout the original record, undermining confidence in these citations.

fabrication_flags: tropical.ventilation: Precise counts (4 dorades, 1 solar vent, 10 ports, 8 hatches) found in no verified source; appears generated.
ergonomics.transom: '32-inch-wide opening' not present in the cited outboundyachts.com cockpit page, which contains no measurements.
ergonomics.mob_recovery: '30-inch lifelines' (taller-than-average) not present in any cited source; appears generated.
heavy_weather.storm_report: '40-knot winds and 15-foot seas' attributed to havewindwilltravel.com, which was fetched and contains no such claim.
stability.stix: 'STIX minimum 38 for Category A' not supported by sailboat-cruising.com source, which gives no minimum threshold.
refit.known_issues: 'Port shaft angle (7-40 degrees down)' is an implausible range for a single hull design; appears to be a hallucinated or garbled figure.
tropical.watermaker: '40 gal/hr' figure contradicted by documented listing showing Spectra Catalina 300 (12.5 gal/hr); may be confused with a different model.

parts.availability: fybmarine.shop URL returns HTTP 404; page does not exist. 'Yammer' misspelling for 'Yanmar' is consistent and systematic throughout the record, suggesting the source material was generated rather than transcribed.
self_steering.autopilot / windvane_option: 'Raymarine Evo' pairing with Outbound 46 not found in any source; cited BWsailing article references Outbound 44, not 46.
verify.verdict: Partially reliable with significant fabrication risk. Core structural facts (ballast weight/type, displacement, LOA/beam, watertight bulkhead location, battery bank composition, mast step drainage issue, rudder moisture, cockpit locker flooding risk) are confirmed by credible sources. However, roughly one-third of specific numerical claims — including ventilation counts, transom width, lifeline height, watermaker flow rate, windvane pairing, storm conditions, STIX threshold, and the prop shaft angle range — are either directly contradicted by the fetched sources or absent from them entirely, and bear hallmarks of AI fabrication (too-precise numbers with no source trail, URLs that 404, systematic misspelling of 'Yanmar' as 'Yammer'). The CE Category A claim is plausible but unconfirmed and the Beaufort/wave thresholds are misquoted. Refit cost ranges are in the right ballpark but the low ends are below what sources actually state. Overall confidence reduced from 0.78 to 0.52 to reflect the volume of unsupported specifics.

cold_score.rationale: Scored over sourced inputs only; insulation (15 pts) and cold tankage (10 pts) are undocumented and excluded from the denominator, leaving 75 sourced points. Earned: heating partial (Isotemp domestic hot-water heater confirmed at 0.55 confidence, but no factory cabin space heater — awarded 30% of 25 = 7.5 pts); inside/sheltered helm confirmed absent at 0.82 (0 pts); keel-stepped mast confirmed at 0.72 (7.2 pts); watertight crash bulkhead confirmed at 0.88 (8.8 pts); hard cockpit shelter (hard dodger) confirmed at 0.82 (8.2 pts). Total earned 31.7 / 75 sourced = 42.3, rounded to 42. Confidence = sourced weight coverage 75/100 = 0.75.
cold_score.basis: heating: Isotemp hot-water heater (Swiftsure listing); inside helm: absent (outboundyachts.com); keel-stepped mast: Cruising World review; watertight bulkhead: Cruising World review; hard dodger: BWsailing review

tropic_score.rationale: SA/D ratio not computable from verified facts (sail area cited 1,083-1,151 sq ft but displacement absent from cleaned_facts), so its 20-point weight is excluded from the denominator, leaving 80 sourced points. Earned: ventilation (opening SS ports and hatches confirmed, but counts were fabricated and removed — awarded 55% x 20 = 11 pts at 0.55 confidence); shade (hard dodger confirmed, canvas awning undocumented — 60% x 15 = 9 pts at 0.60); refrigeration (dual fridge/icebox confirmed, optional mechanical refrigeration at 0.60 — 60% x 15 = 9 pts); watermaker (not factory standard; one owner-fitted Spectra Catalina 300 on one listing — awarded 15% x 15 = 2.25 pts, very low partial); airflow (passive cross-ventilation via raised salon and port/hatch placement at 0.65 — 65% x 15 = 9.75 pts). Total earned 41 / 80 sourced = 51.25, rounded to 51. Confidence = 80/100 sourced coverage = 0.80.
tropic_score.basis: ventilation: BWsailing review; shade: BWsailing review; refrigeration: Swiftsure Yachts listing; watermaker: Swiftsure listing (owner-fit only); airflow: BWsailing review

stability_score.rationale: Neither AVS nor CE category is confirmed in verified sources. AVS was explicitly noted as 'not publicly documented' (confidence 0.2, from a generic design-categories page that does not reference the Outbound 46). CE Category A was removed from verified facts entirely because no fetched source confirmed it and the cited wave/Beaufort thresholds were misquoted. Per rubric: neither AVS nor CE confirmed → value 0, basis 'not published', confidence 0. The encapsulated 10,000 lb bulb keel and watertight crash bulkhead are encouraging structural indicators, but they cannot substitute for a published AVS or CE certification under this rubric.

refit_projection.assumptions: Assumes a median-age example of approximately 14 years (circa 2012 build, purchased 2026). Engine repower excluded: the Yanmar 4JH80 at 14 years is unlikely to require full repower unless high-hours; routine major service is captured under known-issues and owner maintenance budgets. Watermaker included at full new-unit cost because it is not factory standard (verified: absent from spec, one listing shows owner-fitted Spectra Catalina 300). Diesel cabin heater included at full install cost because it is confirmed optional, not factory standard (Isotemp hot-water heater is standard but provides only domestic hot water, not cabin space heating). Ranges are our estimates; low end assumes good survey condition with selective renewal; high end assumes comprehensive offshore-preparation standard with labor at US Pacific/Atlantic coastal rates. Rudder moisture repair not included as a line item because it is confirmed on 'some' vessels but not all; a pre-purchase survey should determine whether it applies.

by_item.note: At or near the 10-15 year inspection/replacement interval for a 14-year-old vessel. Stainless wire with swaged terminals; offshore cruising standard per Cruising World sourced interval.
8-10 year service interval; likely at or past first replacement cycle on a 14-year boat. Halyards, sheets, control lines.
8-12 year service life; at 14 years the original suit is likely past useful offshore life. Outbound 46 sail area 1,083-1,151 sq ft (verified); full cruising suit replacement.
10-15 year replacement cycle; 14-year electronics likely obsolete or failed. Includes chartplotter, AIS, VHF/SSB, autopilot head unit, instruments.
6-10 year cycle; battery bank (1x Group 27 + 3x 8D verified) well past service life at 14 years. Includes bank replacement and solar panel installation/expansion for offshore electrical autonomy.
Standard bluewater preparation; anchor(s), chain, windlass service or replacement.
25-year service life; at 14 years a 2012 boat is approaching mid-life — inspect and replace any stiff or corroded units as part of offshore preparation. Includes backing plates.
Hydrostatic releases, EPIRB registration, liferaft service or replacement on interval. Mandatory for bluewater passage.

by_item.system: Canvas (dodger, bimini, sail covers, cockpit cushions)
by_item.note: 10-15 year canvas life; hard dodger is factory-fitted but soft enclosure, bimini, sail covers, and upholstery likely need renewal on a 14-year boat.

Watermaker is NOT factory standard on the Outbound 46 (verified fact). One documented listing shows a Spectra Catalina 300 as owner-fitted aftermarket. Budget for full new unit plus installation if purchasing a boat without one.

by_item.system: Known Issues: Port Light Sealant, Mast Step Drainage, Cockpit Locker Gasket
by_item.note: Three Outbound 46-specific issues confirmed in verified sources: port light sealant failures on early models ($400-1,200), mast step drainage attention required ($300-800), cockpit locker gasket to prevent bilge flooding ($250-600). Cumulative low/high shown.

by_item.system: Diesel Cabin Heater (cold cruising — not factory standard)
by_item.note: Confirmed optional, not factory standard. Isotemp domestic hot-water heater is standard but provides no cabin space heating. A 4-5 kW diesel cabin heater (e.g., Webasto, Espar) with install is required for cold-water passage readiness.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker — budget for full unit if absent
provisioning_specific.why: Watermaker is verified absent from factory specification. The sole documented example (Spectra Catalina 300, 12.5 gal/hr) was owner-fitted aftermarket. A bluewater passage boat without a watermaker requires provisioning 1-2 gal/person/day for all offshore legs; for a 46-footer with crew of two on a 3,000 nm passage that is 180-360 gal minimum tankage. Verify whether the specific vessel has a unit installed before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel cabin heater — install before high-latitude passage
provisioning_specific.why: Factory standard is an Isotemp domestic hot-water heater only (verified, 0.55 confidence). No diesel space heater is factory-fitted. Cold-water passages (North Atlantic, Pacific Northwest, Southern Ocean approaches) require active cabin heating. Budget $2,500-$4,500 for a 4-5 kW Webasto or Espar unit with installation.

provisioning_specific.item: Inner forestay / storm sail capability — verify or add
provisioning_specific.why: Verified fact: the Outbound 46 is a Solent rig, not a traditional cutter; no fixed inner forestay is standard. Storm jib deployment requires either an existing removable inner forestay provision or aftermarket addition. Confirm hardware before any offshore passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Mast step drainage — inspect and remediate before departure
provisioning_specific.why: Verified in spinsheet.com review (0.72 confidence): mast step drainage requires ongoing attention. Standing water at the mast step can cause osmotic damage to the step laminate and accelerate structural degradation over time. Remediate with epoxy fairing and drainage improvement before a long passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Cockpit locker gasket — replace before offshore use
provisioning_specific.why: Verified in Cruising World review: the port cockpit seat locker opening exposes the bilge to flooding risk in heavy weather. A proper compression gasket (not simple guttering) is required for offshore safety. Low-cost item ($250-600) but high consequence if ignored in breaking-wave conditions.

provisioning_specific.item: Rudder moisture survey — commission pre-purchase ultrasound
provisioning_specific.why: Verified in refit.known_issues (0.72 confidence): rudder interior moisture was detected on surveyed vessels including a two-year-old example, described as 'surprising.' An ultrasonic moisture survey of the rudder should be part of any pre-purchase survey; repair costs range from minor ($500-1,500 seal/drain) to significant ($3,000-8,000 delamination repair) depending on extent.

provisioning_specific.item: Battery bank amp-hour verification — do not assume capacity
provisioning_specific.why: Verified fact (0.65 confidence): factory bank is 1x Group 27 + 3x 8D batteries. Amp-hour total is not documented. Three 8D batteries yield approximately 750-900 Ah at 12V; Group 27 adds ~100 Ah. However, actual state-of-health after years of service is unknown. Load-test the bank before departure and budget for full replacement ($5,000-$20,000 including solar) if more than 6-8 years old.

provisioning_specific.item: Prop shaft pull under power — plan for close-quarters maneuvering
provisioning_specific.why: Verified known issue (0.72 confidence): characteristic pulling under power caused by shaft angle has been noted since hull #1. This is relevant for marina entry/exit, tight anchorages, and MOB maneuvers under power. Factor this into single-handed handling plans; consider bow thruster if not already fitted.
